perturbation.original_text: Recipient will treat all CI with at least the same degree of care as it treats its own information of similar sensitivity, but never with less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will treat all CI with a degree of care it deems appropriate, but never with less than minimal care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the same degree of care as it treats its own information of similar sensitivity, but never with less than reasonable care' to 'a degree of care it deems appropriate, but never with less than minimal care' introduces ambiguity. 'Deems appropriate' is subjective. Replacing 'reasonable care' with 'minimal care' also lowers the standard, potentially violating data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information. The modified text, allowing Recipient to use a degree of care 'it deems appropriate, but never with less than minimal care,' could fall short of the CCPA's requirement for 'reasonable security procedures and practices'.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correct : You may ask businesses to correct inaccurate information that they have about you. Right to limit use and disclosure of sensitive personal information : You can direct businesses to only use your sensitive personal information (for example, your social security number, financial account information, your precise geolocation data, or your genetic data) for limited purposes, such as providing you with the services you requested. You also have the right to be notified, before or at the point businesses collect your personal information, of the types of personal information they are collecting and what they may do with that information. Generally, businesses cannot discriminate against you for exercising your rights under the CCPA. Businesses cannot make you waive these rights, and any contract provision that says you waive these rights is unenforceable. 2. What if I am not a California resident? Only California residents have rights under the CCPA. A California resident is a natural person (as opposed to a corporation or other business entity) who resides in California, even if the person is temporarily outside of the state. 3. What is considered personal information and sensitive personal information under the CCPA? Personal
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a significant weakening of the standard of care required for protecting personal information, potentially violating CCPA. The original text mandated a degree of care equivalent to that used for the recipient's own sensitive information, but never less than reasonable care. The modified text lowers this standard to a degree the recipient 'deems appropriate' but never less than 'minimal care'. This change directly contradicts the intent of CIV 1798.81.5 (a)(1), which seeks to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected and encourages businesses to 'provide reasonable security for that information'. By replacing 'reasonable care' with 'minimal care', the revised contract opens the door for practices that would not meet the standard of 'reasonable security procedures and practices' as described in CIV 1798.81.5 (b).

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI promptly upon request, together with any copies, except as otherwise required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will endeavor to stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI upon request, subject to internal policies, together with readily available copies, except as otherwise required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI promptly upon request' to 'will endeavor to stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI upon request, subject to internal policies' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor' implies an effort but not a guarantee. 'Subject to internal policies' allows Recipient to avoid compliance if their policies dictate otherwise. Limiting the copies to return to only 'readily available copies' also weakens the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA allows for injunctive relief against misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified clause provides loopholes (endeavor, internal policies, readily available copies) that could hinder the Discloser's ability to obtain an injunction, since the Recipient is not strictly obligated to return or destroy all CI.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Title 5. Uniform Trade Secrets Act :: Civil Code :: 2010 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2010 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 California Code Civil Code Title 5. Uniform Trade Secrets Act CIVIL CODE SECTION 3426-3426.11 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances t
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity that weakens the legal obligation to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction. The original text requires the recipient to 'stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI promptly upon request,' while the changed text only requires the recipient to 'endeavor to stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI upon request, subject to internal policies, together with readily available copies'. The addition of 'endeavor,' 'subject to internal policies,' and 'readily available copies' creates loopholes, making it more difficult for the discloser to enforce their rights and obtain injunctive relief. The LLM's law explanation also corroborates this analysis. While the snippets do not directly address these specific weakening terms, they establish the basis for trade secret protection under CUTSA, which is undermined by the ambiguities in the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose CI (A) as approved in a writing signed by Discloser or (B) as necessary to respond to a valid order by a court or other governmental body, as required by law, or as necessary to establish the rights of either party, provided that Recipient promptly notifies Discloser upon receipt of the disclosure order and requests confidential treatment of any affected CI.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose CI (A) as approved in a writing signed by Discloser or (B) as reasonably interpreted as necessary to respond to what it believes to be a valid request by a court or other governmental body, or as necessary to vaguely establish the rights of either party, provided that Recipient attempts to notify Discloser upon receipt of the disclosure request and considers requesting confidential treatment of any affected CI.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying 'as necessary to respond to a valid order by a court or other governmental body, as required by law' to 'as reasonably interpreted as necessary to respond to what it believes to be a valid request by a court or other governmental body' introduces significant ambiguity and allows for subjective interpretation. Replacing 'provided that Recipient promptly notifies Discloser upon receipt of the disclosure order and requests confidential treatment of any affected CI' with 'provided that Recipient attempts to notify Discloser upon receipt of the disclosure request and considers requesting confidential treatment of any affected CI' weakens the notification and confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1985.6 of the California Code of Civil Procedure outlines specific procedures that must be followed when a subpoena seeks the production of an employee's personnel records. These procedures include providing notice to the employee and affording them an opportunity to object. By changing the requirement to 'attempts to notify' and allowing for subjective interpretation of the validity of the request, the modified text conflicts with the employee's right to notice and objection under California law when their personnel records are sought via subpoena. Additionally, requesting treatment of any affected CI is not a strong enough obligation.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE [1985 - 2015.6] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Means of Production [1985 - 1997] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1985.6. (a) For purposes of this section, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) “Deposition officer” means a person who meets the qualifications specified in Section 2020.420. (2) “Employee” means any individual who is or has been employed by a witness subject to a subpoena duces tecum. “Employee” also means any individual who is or has been represented by a labor organization that is a witness subject to a subpoena duces tecum. (3) “Employment records” means the original or any copy of books, documents, other writings, or electronically stored information pertaining to the employment of any employee maintained by the current or former employer of the employee, or by any labor organization that has represented or currently represents the employee. (4) “Labor organization” has the meaning set forth in Section 1117 of the Labor Code. (5) “Subpoenaing party” means the person or persons causing a subpoena duces tecum to be issued or served in connection with any civil action or proceedi
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens obligations related to disclosing confidential information in response to legal requests, potentially conflicting with legal requirements for proper notification and protection of employee records as indicated in CCP 1985.6. The original text requires prompt notification upon receipt of a disclosure order, while the changed text only requires an attempt to notify. Furthermore, the changed text weakens the obligation to request confidential treatment. This weakens the rights of the employee to object as noted in the LLM explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The parties will comply with all applicable export laws and regulations in their use and disclosure of CI.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties will endeavor to comply with what they believe to be the relevant export laws and regulations in their use and disclosure of CI.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates compliance with all applicable export laws. The revised text uses "endeavor," which implies an attempt but not a guarantee of compliance. It also adds "what they believe to be," which makes the determination of which laws are relevant subjective, undermining the obligation to comply with all applicable laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR requires strict compliance with export control regulations. By using "endeavor to comply with what they believe to be the relevant...laws", the modified text contradicts the EAR's requirement for strict compliance, as the parties could argue they believed a certain regulation was not relevant, even if it was.

Enhanced Content :: FR Reference Title 15 This content is from the eCFR and is authoritative but unofficial. Dis
perturbation.justification: The changed text uses "endeavor to comply with what they believe to be the relevant...laws", introducing subjectivity and weakening the obligation to comply with all applicable export laws. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the EAR's requirement for strict compliance, as parties could argue they believed a certain regulation was not relevant, even if it was. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that Title 15 of the eCFR is authoritative but unofficial, which means that while it is not the official document, it contains the laws and regulations related to commerce and foreign trade. The change from "will comply" to "endeavor to comply with what they believe" significantly lowers the standard of compliance, making it easier to argue non-compliance. This creates a legal contradiction because the original contract mandates full compliance, while the modified version allows for subjective interpretation and potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: This NDA may only be modified by mutual written agreement signed by authorized representatives of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This NDA may be subject to modifications, ideally through a written agreement, acknowledged in some form by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear requirement for modifications: a mutual written agreement signed by authorized representatives. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using "may be subject to modifications," which is vague. Replacing the precise requirement of a "written agreement signed by authorized representatives" with "ideally through a written agreement, acknowledged in some form" weakens the requirement, opening the door to potentially invalid modifications.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1698 generally requires that a contract in writing may be altered only by a contract in writing, or by an executed oral agreement. The modified text's allowance for modifications "acknowledged in some form" could be interpreted as permitting oral modifications that are not fully executed, which would be invalid under Section 1698, especially if the original contract explicitly requires written amendments.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates modifications to be in writing and signed by authorized representatives. The changed text introduces ambiguity by suggesting modifications may be acknowledged in 'some form,' potentially including oral agreements. This contradicts California Civil Code Section 1698(a), as stated in the Law Explanation, which generally requires written contracts to be modified by written contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1698(a), explicitly states: 'A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing.' The modified text's allowance for non-written acknowledgment directly undermines this requirement, creating a contradiction.
